content.plain_english: This week your child is comparing how heavy things are and how much liquid containers can hold. They are learning the words heavier, lighter, full and empty.
content.in_school: Children weigh classroom objects on balance scales — a pencil, a book, a small toy. They notice which side goes down (the heavier one). They fill bottles and jars with water to see 'full' and 'empty', and pour water from one container to another to compare volumes.
content.home_activity: At bath time, fill a small cup, a yogurt pot and a jug from the same tap. Ask your child to put them in order from 'holds least' to 'holds most'. Then dry their hands and pick up two toys — which is heavier? Three minutes during a bath is enough.
dinner_table_questions: Which is heavier — your fork or your plate?
Is the milk carton full or empty?
If we pour my glass into yours, will it overflow?

key_vocabulary.definition: Has more mass — pulls down more on a scale.
A container with no more space inside.
A container with nothing in it.
How much space something takes up, or how much a container holds.

example_questions: Which is heavier — an apple or a marble?
Order these from lightest to heaviest: book, pencil, shoe.
If a jug is full, what could you do to make it half full?
content.times_table_tip: Year 1 children practise counting in 2s, 5s and 10s. While filling cups at bath time, count in 2s as you pour — each pour is two more.
